text.title: regulatory-compliance-2026 Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for regulatory-compliance-2026 as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Regulations are jurisdictional; this methodology gives a US/EU snapshot but ignores UK Equality Act, Japanese JIS X 8341, Australian DDA, etc.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: If your product serves Australia, Japan, or UK, research those standards separately. This methodology covers the largest markets (US + EU); add region-specific rules as your user base expands.

failure-mode.description: The compliance checklist is necessary but not sufficient — passes don't preclude lawsuits.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Conformance certification provides evidence of good-faith effort, but does not guarantee immunity. Courts focus on whether the product is actually usable by disabled users, not checkbox compliance.

failure-mode.description: WCAG 2.1 AA is a floor; some agencies (US federal contracts, EU public sector) require 2.2 AA or AAA in places.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: High-risk surfaces (election sites, healthcare portals, financial services) may have stricter requirements. Check your sector's guidance.

failure-mode.description: "Statement published" can be a false-comfort metric — wrong claims of conformance increase legal exposure.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: An accessibility statement claiming WCAG 2.1 AA conformance without evidence of testing is worse than no statement (it's evidence of intent to deceive). Stake claims to actual testing.
